text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- CLINICAL TRIAL FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Gerald T. Holloway | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Investigator, MD] | Fellow: [on file]
Clinical Trial: Phase I/II KRAS G12D-Selective Inhibitor Study
Protocol Visit: Cycle 3, Day 1 (Week 6 on study)
Department: Medical Oncology / Investigational Drug Service

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, BRAF wild-type) presenting for his Cycle 3, Day 1 protocol visit on the Phase I/II trial of an investigational oral KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor. He is approximately 21 months from his initial diagnosis.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago) with a known diagnosis of Stage IVA (pT3 pN2a M1a) moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed approximately 21 months ago. His disease was characterized at diagnosis by KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 41%), MSS status by both IHC and NGS, BRAF wild-type, NRAS wild-type, and co-mutations in APC and TP53. HER2 was not amplified. TMB was low (6 mut/Mb). At diagnosis he had synchronous liver metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI).

He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis approximately 5 weeks before starting systemic therapy, achieving an R0 resection (pT3 pN2a, 3/18 lymph nodes positive, lymphovascular and perineural invasion present). The liver metastases were left in situ at the time of surgery.

First-line systemic therapy: He was treated with modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab, initiated approximately 16 months ago. He achieved a partial response to FOLFOX/bev, with his four hepatic lesions shrinking considerably (segment VI from 3.1 cm to as small as 1.5 cm at best response). However, he developed cumulative oxaliplatin-related peripheral sensory neuropathy that ultimately progressed to grade 2-3 functional impairment, prompting discontinuation of oxaliplatin after cycle 10. He was then transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab. Neuropathy began to gradually improve off oxaliplatin. Hypertension was also a notable toxicity, requiring uptitration of his antihypertensive regimen from lisinopril monotherapy to triple-agent therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Approximately 10 months from treatment initiation, restaging CT demonstrated progression -- slight growth in hepatic lesions and the emergence of 2 new right-sided pulmonary nodules, with a concurrent rise in CEA from 22 to 41 ng/mL. His nadir CEA on first-line therapy had been approximately 22 ng/mL.

Second-line systemic therapy: He was switched to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 10.5 months from initial treatment start. He again achieved a partial response to this regimen, with both hepatic and pulmonary lesions responding. He tolerated the regimen with manageable toxicity -- grade 2 diarrhea controlled with loperamide, grade 2 alopecia (expected with irinotecan), and grade 3 hypertension during cycles 8-9 that required a brief hold on ramucirumab and further antihypertensive intensification (amlodipine increased from 5 mg to 10 mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added). UGT1A1 genotyping showed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer). Disease remained in control for approximately 9 months on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Restaging CT at approximately 19 months from initial diagnosis demonstrated disease progression -- slow but definite growth in hepatic lesions (aggregate about 23% increase from nadir) and growth of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule from 0.7 cm to 0.9 cm, with rising CEA (22 ng/mL nadir rising to 58 ng/mL). No new metastatic sites were identified.

At that point, given KRAS G12D mutation, the patient remained ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). His MSS status precluded immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. He was referred to the investigational drug service and underwent screening for the current Phase I/II trial of an oral, selective KRAS G12D inhibitor. Re-biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule confirmed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (CK20+, CDX2+, TTF-1 negative) with preserved KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 38%) and no new acquired resistance mutations (NRAS, BRAF, KRAS amplification all negative), confirming ongoing KRAS G12D-driven disease. He enrolled approximately 1.5 months ago and is currently completing his third cycle of the investigational agent.

He reports that overall, he has tolerated the oral investigational agent acceptably. He describes a mild, diffuse skin rash that appeared during week 2 of treatment, which was managed with topical clindamycin gel applied twice daily and has improved. He notes mild nausea, primarily in the first 1-2 hours after taking the morning dose, which he manages by taking the medication with a small amount of food. He has not required any dose interruptions. He denies vomiting, significant diarrhea, oral mucositis, or dyspnea. He notes mild fatigue, which he rates as 3/10 and considers manageable -- he has been able to perform light household activities and short walks. His residual neuropathy, primarily numbness in the soles of his feet, has continued to slowly improve off oxaliplatin and is now grade 1 and non-limiting. He notes his appetite is fair; he has lost approximately 2-3 pounds since starting this trial, which he attributes to mild nausea rather than anorexia. He has been monitoring his blood pressure at home and reports readings in the range of 130-140/84-90 mmHg on his current triple antihypertensive regimen.

He is hopeful. He mentions that his CEA has been heading in a better direction and is curious about the upcoming CT scan results.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (grade 1), stable. Mild nausea, primarily perioperative to morning dosing, well controlled. Appetite fair; 2-3 lb weight loss. No fever, chills, or night sweats.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headache, visual changes, epistaxis, or oral sores.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension as noted, home blood pressure monitoring ongoing. No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis. (Incidental pulmonary nodules under surveillance on trial imaging.)
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea as above. Bowel movements regular, formed, 1-2 per day. No diarrhea. No abdominal pain, hematochezia, or change in stool caliber. No symptoms at sigmoid resection site.
Musculoskeletal/Neurologic: Grade 1 residual peripheral sensory neuropathy, bilateral feet, continuing to improve slowly. No new neurologic complaints. No bone pain, joint pain, or muscle weakness.
Integumentary: Mild, improving acneiform/follicular rash on face and upper chest, grade 1, on topical clindamycin. No blistering, pruritus, or involvement of mucous membranes.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary frequency changes.
Endocrine/Metabolic: No new symptoms.
Hematologic: No unusual bruising, bleeding, or petechiae.
Psychiatric: Mild anxiety related to disease status (appropriate). Mood generally improved with early marker response on trial. Denies depression or hopelessness.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing 59-year-old male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears fatigued but not cachectic.
Vitals: Blood pressure 138/87 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 184 lbs (down approximately 2 lbs from last visit 6 weeks ago), height 5'11", BSA 2.08 m2, ECOG performance status 1.
Skin: Mild, scattered papulopustular rash over bilateral cheeks, forehead, and upper chest, grade 1 in severity, consistent with class effect from KRAS pathway inhibition. No open lesions, no superinfection noted. No palmar-plantar erythema.
Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclera non-icteric. Oropharynx moist, no mucositis or ulceration. Neck supple, no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.
Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Equal air entry.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed laparoscopic port sites from prior sigmoid colectomy. No hepatomegaly palpated. No splenomegaly. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present in all quadrants.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Mild bilateral symmetric diminished sensation to light touch on the plantar surfaces of both feet (consistent with grade 1 residual neuropathy). Normal muscle bulk and strength bilaterally. Reflexes intact.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait: slightly cautious, non-ataxic, independently ambulatory without assistive device.
Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or mediastinal adenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today per protocol)

Complete Blood Count:
  White blood cell count: 5.2 x10^3/uL (reference 4.0-11.0)
  Absolute neutrophil count: 3,100/uL (adequate; no dose modification criteria met)
  Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL (low; mild anemia of chronic disease, similar to prior)
  Hematocrit: 31.8%
  Platelets: 228 x10^3/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  Sodium: 139 mEq/L | Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L | CO2: 26 mEq/L | BUN: 14 mg/dL | Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
  Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL | Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
  ALT: 44 U/L (mildly elevated, similar to prior -- chronic elevation in setting of hepatic metastases)
  AST: 41 U/L (mildly elevated, similar trend)
  Alkaline phosphatase: 102 U/L (mildly elevated)
  Albumin: 3.7 g/dL
  Total protein: 6.8 g/dL
  Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL | Glucose: 97 mg/dL

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal)

Tumor Markers:
  CEA: 42 ng/mL (prior at trial enrollment: 58 ng/mL; continued decline from progression peak)
  CA 19-9: 35 U/mL (prior at trial enrollment: 47 U/mL; continued declining trend)

Urinalysis: Trace protein, no hematuria, no significant abnormality.

Protocol-specific safety labs: Creatine kinase within normal limits. Lipase within normal limits. Thyroid function tests pending (per protocol monitoring).

ECG (per protocol): Normal sinus rhythm. QTc 432 ms (within acceptable range per protocol). No new conduction abnormalities.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast was obtained as part of the protocol-specified cycle 3 tumor assessment and is reviewed below (formal radiology read on file; my interpretation summarized here):

Liver: The four known hepatic metastatic lesions show a favorable trend compared to the most recent imaging prior to trial enrollment (at which time the liver lesions had progressed on second-line therapy). Segment VI lesion now measures approximately 1.2 cm (was 1.5 cm at trial entry and 1.3 cm at best response on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab nadir). Segment IVb lesion now approximately 0.8 cm (was 1.2 cm at trial entry). Segment II lesion: 0.5 cm (stable to mildly decreased, was 0.6 cm). Segment VII lesion: 0.3 cm (stable, was 0.4 cm). Overall the hepatic burden looks notably better than it did at the time of enrollment, and frankly better than the best response he achieved on second-line therapy -- encouraging at just 6 weeks.

Lungs: The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule now measures approximately 0.6 cm, down from 0.9 cm at trial entry. The right upper lobe nodule measures 0.4 cm (down from 0.5 cm). Both pulmonary nodules are smaller than the trial entry measurements. No new pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusions. No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen/Pelvis: The sigmoid resection site remains unremarkable without evidence of anastomotic recurrence or local disease. No peritoneal implants are identified. No ascites. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy: none significant. Adrenal glands normal. Kidneys unremarkable.

Bone windows: No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions.

Overall impression: Meaningful tumor shrinkage in both hepatic and pulmonary lesions at 6 weeks, representing a preliminary response to the investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor. The degree of shrinkage exceeds what would be expected from natural tumor kinetics at this interval and is consistent with early drug activity. This result, combined with the declining tumor markers, is encouraging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type -- on Phase I/II investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor trial, Cycle 3.

Disease status: The patient is showing a promising early response to the investigational agent. Both his hepatic and pulmonary lesions have decreased in size compared to trial enrollment imaging, and his tumor markers (CEA down from 58 to 42 ng/mL; CA 19-9 down from 47 to 35 U/mL) have continued to trend downward over the past 6 weeks. This is his first KRAS-targeted therapy and the response -- while early -- is encouraging given the absence of therapeutic options after two lines of standard cytotoxic/antiangiogenic therapy. He has tolerated the oral investigational agent well without need for dose interruption or modification so far. He will proceed to Cycle 4 at the assigned dose level per protocol.

I reviewed the imaging personally with the patient today. He was visibly relieved and encouraged by the preliminary results. I cautioned him that it is early and that the durability of this response is not yet known, but that we are cautiously optimistic. We will continue to reassess at each protocol-specified imaging timepoint.

2. Toxicity management:
   a. Acneiform rash, grade 1: Continue topical clindamycin 1% gel BID. Patient instructed to maintain facial sun protection (SPF 30+), as photosensitivity can worsen rash. No systemic antibiotics required at this time. If rash progresses to grade 2 or higher, will initiate doxycycline per protocol-specified toxicity management guidelines.
   b. Nausea, grade 1: Persistent but mild and manageable. Patient taking the investigational drug with a small meal. Ondansetron 4 mg ODT available as PRN rescue and patient uses it approximately 2-3 times per week. No dose modifications required for grade 1 nausea. Will reassess.
   c. Fatigue, grade 1: Counseled on energy conservation techniques, maintaining light physical activity as tolerated, adequate nutrition. No anemia-specific intervention at this time given Hgb 10.5 g/dL and otherwise stable; will monitor. Not meeting threshold for erythropoiesis-stimulating agent use or transfusion per current parameters.
   d. Peripheral neuropathy, grade 1 (residual from prior oxaliplatin): Stable and slowly improving. Not attributable to current investigational agent based on mechanism. No additional intervention required. Continue monitoring.

3. Hypertension, grade 1-2: Blood pressure 138/87 mmHg today, well-controlled on current triple antihypertensive regimen (lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily). Ramucirumab was the primary driver of severe hypertension on prior regimen; the current investigational KRAS inhibitor is not primarily antiangiogenic. Home blood pressure monitoring continued at twice-daily frequency. No changes to antihypertensive regimen today. If BP exceeds 150/95 mmHg on repeat checks, will reassess; may consider uptitration or cardiology consultation.

4. Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 44, AST 41 U/L): Chronically mildly elevated in setting of hepatic metastases, and unchanged from prior visits. Total bilirubin normal. No evidence of hepatotoxicity attributable to the investigational agent at this time. Will continue to monitor per protocol. If LFTs escalate (>3x ULN), will review protocol-specified dose modification criteria and consider hepatology consultation.

5. Mild anemia, Hgb 10.5 g/dL: Consistent with anemia of chronic disease in a patient on active cancer therapy. No transfusion threshold met. No iron deficiency features on prior labs. Will continue to monitor with each cycle CBC.

6. Goals of care: Patient remains fully engaged in treatment with curative/disease-control intent. He understands this is an investigational therapy and that the trial is early phase. He has been informed of the right to withdraw at any time. He continues to live independently at home with his wife, who accompanies him to visits. He is maintaining his baseline activities of daily living with grade 1 functional limitations.

7. Follow-up: Return for Cycle 4, Day 1 in 2 weeks per protocol. All mandatory safety labs and ECG will be repeated at that visit. Protocol-specified Cycle 3 tumor marker labs reviewed today. Full restaging CT next due at Cycle 6 per protocol schedule (approximately 6 weeks from today). Patient instructed to contact the trial coordinator or on-call oncology team immediately for fever >100.4°F, severe diarrhea, significant dyspnea, new skin blistering, eye symptoms, or any other grade 3 or higher adverse event.

Time spent with patient: approximately 40 minutes. More than half of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

[Signed electronically]
[Attending Oncologist / Investigator, MD]
Medical Oncology / Investigational Drug Service
